title: Executive Dysfunction Explained: The 7 Functions and ADHD
description: Executive dysfunction is not laziness, bad habits, or lack of willpower. It is a neurological difference in how the ADHD brain manages the seven core executive functions — and understanding each one changes everything. This video breaks down the real science behind why starting tasks, managing time, controlling emotions, and following through feel so much harder with ADHD. Learn what is actually happening in your brain and what strategies are built for how it actually works.

chapters.title: The Real Problem Has a Name
chapters.voiceover: You have had the same email open for forty minutes. You know exactly what it says. You know exactly what you need to write back. And you cannot make yourself do it. Not because you do not care. Not because you are lazy. Because the part of your brain responsible for initiating that action is not firing the way it should. That is not a character flaw. That is executive dysfunction. And if nobody has ever explained it properly to you, that changes today. Executive function is the set of mental processes that lets you plan, start, sustain, and complete goal-directed behavior. Think of it less like a skill and more like the operating system your brain runs on. In ADHD, that operating system has a fundamentally different architecture. The prefrontal cortex, the region most responsible for executive control, shows reduced activation and delayed development in ADHD brains. We are talking measurable differences on brain scans. Not a character deficit. Neurology. Researchers like Russell Barkley have identified seven distinct executive functions, and ADHD affects every single one of them, just in different ways and to different degrees for each person. This video is going to walk through all seven, one by one. What each one actually does, what it looks like when it breaks down in daily life, and what actually helps. Not platitudes. Real strategies. Let's start.

chapters.title: Working Memory: The Broken Whiteboard
chapters.voiceover: Working memory is your brain's temporary workspace. It is where you hold information in mind while you use it. You walk into a room to get something. By the time you get there, you have lost what it was. You are mid-sentence and the second half disappears. Someone gives you three instructions and you retain one and a half. That is not stupidity. That is working memory dysfunction. In neurotypical brains, the prefrontal cortex keeps information actively rehearsed and available through sustained neural firing. Research shows that in ADHD brains, this sustained firing is weaker and less consistent. The signal fades faster. Imagine a whiteboard that erases itself every thirty seconds. You are not bad at remembering things. Your whiteboard just has a faster fade rate. Common advice says make a list. That is actually helpful, but only if the strategy goes all the way. Here is how to make it actually work for an ADHD brain: externalize everything the moment it enters your head, not later. The strategy is called a capture system. One trusted place, frictionless to access, phone notes or a pocket notebook, that you check every morning. Not a complex app. Not a system that requires setup. Just one place, always open, always with you, that you offload to immediately. The deeper fix is reducing the demands on working memory in the first place. Break instructions into one-step-at-a-time actions. Read one line, do one thing, come back. Your working memory is not broken. It is just smaller than you need it to be, and once you stop fighting that and start designing around it, everything shifts.

chapters.title: Response Inhibition: When the Brain Cannot Hit Pause
chapters.voiceover: Response inhibition is the ability to pause before you act. To stop yourself from blurting something out. To resist the impulse to check your phone right now. To not say yes to three things you have no time for. It sounds simple. For the ADHD brain, it is genuinely hard, and here is why. The braking system of the brain runs through the right inferior frontal cortex and the subthalamic nucleus. Neuroimaging studies consistently show reduced volume and activity in these regions in ADHD. The brake is there. It just has less pressure behind it. And this is why willpower advice is not just unhelpful, it is structurally wrong. Telling someone with ADHD to just think before they act is like telling someone with a sprained ankle to just walk normally. The mechanism itself is impaired. What this looks like in real life: you are in a meeting and a thought arrives and it feels urgent, it feels like it will disappear if you do not say it immediately, so you say it, and then you watch the social consequence land in real time. Or you send a message you regret seventeen seconds later. Or you buy something you did not need because the impulse arrived and the pause did not. A practical strategy here is what is sometimes called an if-then rule, written out in advance. If I feel the urge to interrupt, then I write it down instead. If I get a purchase impulse, then I wait twenty-four hours before buying. These pre-committed rules offload the inhibition work from your in-the-moment willpower, which is unreliable, and into a system you set up when your brain was calm. That is not a workaround. That is brain-aligned design.

chapters.title: Time Blindness and Emotional Self-Regulation
chapters.voiceover: These two executive functions get their own chapter together because they are deeply connected and because both of them carry enormous shame. Time blindness is not a metaphor. Barkley describes ADHD as fundamentally a disorder of time perception. Neurotypical brains have a continuous, intuitive sense of time passing. The ADHD brain lives in two time zones: now and not now. If the deadline is three weeks away, it does not feel real yet. If something needs to happen in forty-five minutes, those forty-five minutes feel like infinite space until suddenly they do not exist anymore. This is why urgency is so activating for ADHD brains. It is not that you work better under pressure because you like stress. It is that urgency converts something from not now into now, which is the only category the brain can truly act on. Practical fix: create artificial now. A timer for twenty-five minutes that is visible, not just audible. Scheduling tasks as appointments with start times in your calendar, not just due dates. Telling someone else your deadline so the social reality makes it feel present. Now, emotional dysregulation. The ADHD brain processes emotional stimuli more intensely and has a weaker inhibitory brake on that response. Something unfair happens and the anger is immediate, massive, and feels permanent. Rejection, even small or ambiguous, hits like a verdict. This is not sensitivity as a personality trait. It is neurological. What helps is not suppression, which does not work anyway, but pause and label. When the emotion arrives, name it out loud or in writing: I am feeling rejection right now. Labeling activates the prefrontal cortex and marginally reduces amygdala activation. You are literally giving your reasoning brain a foothold back in.

chapters.title: Planning, Initiation, and Self-Monitoring
chapters.voiceover: We are covering the final three executive functions now, and these three are where ADHD is most often misread as laziness or avoidance. Planning is the ability to think ahead, sequence steps, and organize a path toward a future goal. Initiation is the ability to start. Self-monitoring is the ability to notice how you are doing and adjust while you are doing it. These are three separate things, and ADHD can impair each of them independently. Planning first. The ADHD brain struggles to hold a sequence of future steps in mind simultaneously, partly due to working memory limitations and partly due to weaker prospective memory, which is the ability to remember to do things in the future. A project that requires fifteen steps before completion does not naturally appear to the ADHD brain as fifteen steps. It appears as one enormous vague blob. The strategy: never plan in your head. Sit down, open a document, and write every single step you can think of. Then ask yourself: is each step actually one action, or is it secretly multiple actions? Make a call is often really find the number, decide what to say, then make the call. Break it down until every item on the list is a single physical action. Now, initiation. This is the most misunderstood one. The ADHD brain does not withhold effort because the person does not care. Dopamine is the neurotransmitter most involved in motivation and the anticipation of reward. In ADHD, the dopamine system is underactive at baseline, which means low-stimulation tasks, anything without novelty, urgency, or immediate payoff, simply do not generate enough signal to start. The brain is not saying no. It is saying there is not enough here to move. Body doubling works here. So does the two-minute rule: commit only to starting for two minutes. Not finishing. Starting. Because once the task is active, the brain often finds enough momentum to continue. Self-monitoring is the quietest of the three. It is the background process that checks: am I on track, have I gone off task, is this working. In ADHD, that background check runs less reliably. You look up and forty minutes have passed and you do not know where they went. Building external check-in points, a recurring two-minute timer, an end-of-hour review habit, gives your self-monitoring function the external scaffold it cannot always generate internally.

chapters.title: This Is Your Brain. Design Around It.
chapters.voiceover: Here is the thing nobody tells you clearly enough. Executive dysfunction does not mean you cannot do these things. It means you cannot do them the same way, with the same tools, on the same timeline as someone whose brain generates its own scaffolding automatically. You have been trying to run software on hardware it was not designed for and blaming yourself for the crashes. Every strategy in this video works on the same principle: externalize the function your brain is not reliably providing internally. Working memory failing? Capture system outside your head. Inhibition unreliable? Pre-committed if-then rules made when you were calm. Time blindness? Visible timers, calendar appointments, artificial deadlines with real social weight. Emotional flooding? Label it to name it, pause to let the prefrontal cortex back in. Planning breaking down? Never plan in your head. Initiation not firing? Lower the bar to two minutes, use body doubling, create novelty or urgency. Self-monitoring drifting? Build external check-in points. None of these strategies require more willpower. They require smarter architecture. They work because they match the actual brain, not the brain someone assumed you have. And one more thing that matters a lot. Shame is not a motivator for an ADHD brain. Research on emotional dysregulation in ADHD consistently shows that shame and self-criticism increase avoidance, not effort. Understanding why your brain works the way it does is not an excuse. It is the starting point for actually changing your outcomes. You are not a lazy person who needs to try harder. You are a person whose executive system is wired differently, and now you know what each piece of that actually means. Build your life around the brain you have. It will surprise you. If this helped make sense of things, follow for daily ADHD content. There is a lot more where this came from.
